Pleiades.notes: Young open cluster in Taurus, ~100 Myr old, ~136 pc away. The nine named 'Seven Sisters' — Alcyone, Atlas, Electra, Maia, Merope, Taygeta, Celaeno, Pleione, and Asterope — are hot blue-white B-type stars glowing through residual reflection nebulosity. One of the most striking sights in a small telescope or binoculars.

Hyades.notes: Nearest open cluster to Sol at ~47 pc, forming the V-shaped face of Taurus. Aldebaran lies in the foreground and is NOT a member — it's less than half the cluster's distance. Age ~625 Myr, roughly 400 confirmed members.

Alpha Persei Cluster.notes: Young (~50 Myr) open cluster centered on the supergiant Mirfak, at ~172 pc. A loose, large association of hot B-type stars visible to the naked eye as a string of stars arcing through Perseus.

Coma Star Cluster.notes: Nearby open cluster in Coma Berenices at ~85 pc. Age ~450 Myr. One of the closest clusters to Sol, forming a loose scattering of 5th-magnitude stars visible to the naked eye.

IC 2391.notes: Young (~50 Myr) open cluster in Vela at ~150 pc, centered on the bright star Omicron Velorum. Visible to the naked eye from the southern hemisphere.

IC 2602.notes: Bright open cluster in Carina at ~150 pc. Often called the 'Southern Pleiades' for its visual resemblance. Age ~30 Myr, one of the youngest nearby clusters.

Beehive Cluster.notes: Large open cluster in Cancer at ~183 pc, visible as a fuzzy patch to the naked eye. Age ~600 Myr. One of the nearest Messier objects, known since antiquity.

NGC 2451A.notes: Nearer component of the NGC 2451 pair in Puppis at ~190 pc. Two unrelated open clusters overlap on the sky; this foreground cluster is younger (~50 Myr) and sparser.

NGC 2451B.notes: Background component of the NGC 2451 pair in Puppis at ~368 pc. A richer, older (~200 Myr) cluster that overlaps the nearer NGC 2451A on the sky but is physically unrelated.

Blanco 1.notes: Open cluster in Sculptor at ~234 pc. Age ~100 Myr. A relatively sparse cluster in the southern sky.

Ptolemy Cluster.notes: Large bright open cluster in Scorpius at ~276 pc. Known since antiquity — Ptolemy cataloged it in 130 AD. Age ~200 Myr. One of the most prominent open clusters in the sky.

IC 4665.notes: Young (~40 Myr) open cluster in Ophiuchus at ~343 pc. A loose, large cluster visible in binoculars.

NGC 2547.notes: Young (~35 Myr) open cluster in Vela at ~382 pc. A rich southern-hemisphere cluster.

NGC 2516.notes: Rich open cluster in Carina at ~407 pc, sometimes called the 'Southern Beehive'. Visible to the naked eye. Age ~150 Myr, with ~3800 confirmed members.

Butterfly Cluster.notes: Open cluster in Scorpius at ~452 pc. Named for its butterfly-like shape. Age ~100 Myr. Visible to the naked eye near the stinger of Scorpius.

NGC 2422.notes: Open cluster in Puppis at ~468 pc. A bright, loose cluster visible to the naked eye under good conditions. Age ~80 Myr.

Wishing Well Cluster.notes: Rich open cluster in Carina at ~472 pc. One of the brightest open clusters in the sky with ~3400 confirmed members. Age ~300 Myr. Named for its resemblance to scattered silver coins.

NGC 6633.notes: Open cluster in Ophiuchus at ~390 pc. A moderately rich cluster visible in binoculars. Age ~600 Myr. Paired with IC 4756 ('Tweedledum and Tweedledee').

IC 4756.notes: Open cluster in Serpens at ~467 pc. Paired with NGC 6633 as 'Tweedledum and Tweedledee' — two similar-age clusters at similar distances in adjacent constellations. Age ~800 Myr, with ~700 confirmed members.

NGC 2287.notes: Open cluster in Canis Major at ~715 pc, about 4° south of Sirius. Contains several red giants. Age ~200 Myr.

NGC 6281.notes: Open cluster in Scorpius at ~521 pc, near μ Scorpii (Xamidimura). Age ~300 Myr, with ~750 confirmed members. Visible in binoculars as a compact knot of stars in the tail of the Scorpion.

NGC 2168.notes: Large open cluster in Gemini at ~839 pc, visible to the naked eye as a hazy spot near Castor's foot. Age ~175 Myr.

Collinder 69.notes: Young open cluster at ~394 pc surrounding λ Orionis, embedded in a ring-shaped HII region. Age ~5 Myr, with ~1200 confirmed members.

NGC 1980.notes: Open cluster at ~378 pc centered on Hatysa (ι Ori). Overlaps with the Orion Nebula Cluster on the sky. Age ~5 Myr.

Sigma Orionis Cluster.notes: Young cluster at ~396 pc around the multiple star σ Orionis. Age ~3 Myr, important for studying brown dwarfs and protoplanetary disks.

ASCC 125.notes: Young subgroup of the Cepheus OB3 association at ~828 pc. Embedded in the Cepheus OB3 molecular cloud, an active region of sequential star formation.

NGC 6124.notes: Prominent open cluster in Scorpius at ~630 pc, visible to the naked eye (mag 5.8). Discovered by Lacaille in 1751. Contains ~125 bright members. Age ~140 Myr.

Stock 2.notes: Large open cluster in Cassiopeia at ~374 pc, sometimes called the Muscleman Cluster. One of the largest nearby clusters with over 3,000 Gaia DR3 members. Age ~150-300 Myr.

Collinder 350.notes: Open cluster in Ophiuchus at ~372 pc, near IC 4665. Age ~500 Myr, older than its neighbor.
